findings.title: Lost-update race in check_and_record read-modify-write of breaker.json
findings.description: check_and_record performs load-modify-write of $TASKQ_HOME/breaker.json without a per-file lock. Under run --all's ThreadPoolExecutor fan-out, racing workers each read the prior failure_count, locally increment, and the last writer wins. With threshold=10 and 20 concurrent failures observation shows failure_count=5 (15 lost); the breaker stays CLOSED while 20 failures already happened.
findings.code_snippet:
def check_and_record(
    success: bool, *, now_fn=time.monotonic
) -> Decision:
    data = _load_breaker()
    state = data.get("state", "CLOSED")
    count = int(data.get("failure_count", 0))
    opened_at = data.get("opened_at")
    now = now_fn()
findings.reasoning: Reproducible: with TASKQ_BREAKER_THRESHOLD=10 and 20 parallel `false` subprocesses via run_all, breaker.json ended with failure_count=5 (vs expected 20). The 3 sequential FR-03 tests at test_fr03.py:208,230 used a for-loop and never exercised the race. PRODUCTION TRIGGER: any operator running `taskq run --all` against >threshold failing tasks will see the breaker silently fail to trip, with no error -- defeating FR-03's safety contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Add threading.Lock() module-local _breaker_lock; wrap the load-modify-write critical section in `with _breaker_lock:` (mirrors the existing cache.py pattern for cache.json).
findings.verify_evidence: Empirical test (run_all w/ 20 concurrent `false` cmds, threshold=10) produced failure_count=5 on unpatched code (15 of 20 failures lost). 2 RED repro tests at tests/test_bug_hunt_breaker_race.py fail RED without the lock; same tests pass GREEN after wrapping check_and_record in `with _breaker_lock:`. All 55 existing tests still pass.

findings.title: add_task name uniqueness lacks lock (theoretical only)
findings.description: add_task loads tasks.json, checks conflict against a local dict, then writes back. Two concurrent calls with the same --name can both pass the conflict check before either writes.
findings.code_snippet:
tasks = _load_tasks()
if name is not None and name != "" and _name_conflict(tasks, name):
    raise ValidationError(...)
task_id = uuid4().hex[:8]
record = {...}
tasks[task_id] = record
_atomic_write_tasks(tasks)
findings.reasoning: Stress-tested in 5 trials with 5 concurrent add_task(name='conflict') under threading.Barrier; only 1 task was created every time. The atomic_write_tasks + os.replace pattern combined with the speed of in-process dict access made the race window effectively un-triggerable. add_task is CLI-typical single-user invocation; no IPC contention from production.
findings.suggested_fix: Optional: add a threading.Lock() around add_task's load-conflict-write if multi-writer CLI processes are ever expected. Not required for current usage.
findings.verify_evidence: 5-trial barrier test under 5-way contention consistently produced 1 task (no observed race). Outcome: refuted as not currently reachable.

resolution.refute_evidence: 5-trial stress test with 5 concurrent add_task(name='conflict') under threading.Barrier consistently created exactly 1 task in tasks.json (no race observed). Single-process CLI usage makes the race window unreachable in practice.

findings.title: Stuck-OPEN fallback when opened_at is None
findings.description: Line 140: if state==OPEN but opened_at is None (defensively possible), `elapsed = now - now = 0`, so the cooldown check `elapsed >= _COOLDOWN` is False, treating the breaker as forever cooling down. The breaker can never recover without manual file deletion.
findings.code_snippet:
if state == "OPEN":
    elapsed = now - (opened_at if opened_at is not None else now)
    if elapsed >= _COOLDOWN:
        data["state"] = "HALF_OPEN"
        data["failure_count"] = count
        _atomic_write_breaker(data)
        return "probe"
    _atomic_write_breaker(data)
    return "reject"
findings.reasoning: Defensive branch: the only code path that sets state='OPEN' (line 180: `data["opened_at"] = now`) also writes opened_at. Reaching OPEN with opened_at=None requires a hand-edited breaker.json or external file munging -- not a production-internal path. Documentation/observability only.
findings.suggested_fix: Optional: surface a logged warning or auto-recover by setting opened_at=now on detected None. Not blocking; treat as hardening.
findings.verify_evidence: No code path sets state='OPEN' without also setting opened_at; reachable only via external JSON munging. Outcome: refuted as production-unreachable.

resolution.refute_evidence: Only state='OPEN' writer (line 180) sets opened_at=now in the same statement. The defensive `opened_at if opened_at is not None else now` branch is unreachable via the public API; only externally-munged breaker.json could trigger it.

findings.title: Defensive isinstance callable-result branch is dead code in production
findings.description: Lines 244-245: `if not isinstance(result, ExecutionResult) and callable(result): result = result()`. _run_once always returns ExecutionResult directly; this branch fires only under deferred-call mock patterns. It is a test-fixture bridge, not a production code path.
findings.code_snippet:
result = _run_once(command, timeout)
if not isinstance(result, ExecutionResult) and callable(result):
    result = result()
_bump_test_attempt_counter()
findings.reasoning: Defensive-but-defensive: only test fixtures (mock.patch.object(executor, "_run_once", return_value=<callable>)) can produce a non-ExecutionResult. In production _run_once returns ExecutionResult unconditionally. The branch is documented and intentional but adds a code-path that mocks the real semantics.
findings.suggested_fix: Cosmetic: rename to `_run_once_result_unwrap` and split into a helper for clarity. No functional bug.
findings.verify_evidence: All production call paths through execute/_run_once return ExecutionResult; the callable-result pattern appears solely in fr03 test mocks. Behaviour under production load is correct.

resolution.refute_evidence: _run_once unconditionally returns ExecutionResult in production (verified by reading lines 151-194); the `isinstance(result, ExecutionResult)` branch only fires under test mocking deferred-call patterns. No production path produces a callable.

findings.title: Bare `except Exception` swallows handler errors silently
findings.description: Line 251-260 wraps _run_once in except Exception; line 269 wraps breaker.check_and_record in except Exception. These can mask bugs (e.g., KeyboardInterrupt after # pragma: no cover would still trigger). However, BaseException is the only truly-noisy class, and these branches preserve user-visible reliability.
findings.reasoning: Per SPEC §7, executor's contract is to never let surprises escape. KeyboardInterrupt + SystemExit are subclasses of BaseException, not Exception, so they correctly propagate. Verified: a non-existent command path returns ExecutionResult(status='failed', exit_code=None, stderr_tail='unexpected error: FileNotFoundError...') as designed.
findings.suggested_fix: Pattern is intentional for CLI reliability; no fix needed. Optionally log caught exceptions to a sidecar for observability.
findings.verify_evidence: Empirical: `taskq.execute('this-command-does-not-exist-xyz')` returns ExecutionResult(status='failed', exit_code=None, stderr_tail='unexpected error: FileNotFoundError...') -- exactly as the contract specifies.

resolution.refute_evidence: Exception (not BaseException) correctly excludes KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit; missing-command returns the documented status='failed' shape. Pattern is intentional per SPEC §7.

findings.title: argparse --json attached to both top-level parser and subparser via shared parent
findings.description: _build_parser attaches `common` (with --json) both to the top-level ArgumentParser AND to each subparser. argparse allows this duplicate, but the option appears twice in the help/usage surface.
findings.code_snippet:
common = argparse.ArgumentParser(add_help=False)
common.add_argument("--json", action="store_true", ...)
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(prog="taskq", parents=[common])
sub = parser.add_subparsers(dest="cmd", required=True)
p_submit = sub.add_parser("submit", parents=[common], ...)
findings.reasoning: Tested with parser.parse_args for all 3 positions: ['--json','submit','echo hi'], ['submit','--json','echo hi'], ['submit','echo hi','--json']. All three parse successfully; --json reaches args.json correctly. argparse silently allows the duplication.
findings.suggested_fix: No fix needed; works correctly. Cosmetic: could deduplicate to a single parent per subparser.
findings.verify_evidence: Empirical argparse test confirms all 3 positions of --json parse correctly with no errors or duplicate option warnings.

resolution.refute_evidence: argparse accepts all 3 positions of --json (before subcommand, between sub+positional, after positional). Verified by direct parser.parse_args invocation; no SystemExit, no duplicate-option error.
